infobox.value: (**APEX**) was a [British](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") which represented [clerical](/wiki/Clerk "Clerk") and [administrative](/wiki/Business_administration "Business administration") employees.

was formed in 1890 and later was renamed as the **National Union of Clerks**. Then, following rapid growth and amalgamation with several other unions, the name was again changed to the **National Union of Clerks and Administrative Workers** (**NUCAW**) with a membership of around 40,000.

(**CAWU**). The union organised in the [white-collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") sector in the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") and across the country, and had particular success in recruiting in the [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") industry. In the 1960s its membership grew rapidly, but it was less successful in the 1970s, membership increasing by 18%, while that of its rival, the [Association of Scientific, Technical and Managerial Staffs](/wiki/Association_of_Scientific,_Technical_and_Managerial_Staffs "Association of Scientific, Technical and Managerial Staffs") (ASTMS), nearly doubled.[[1]](#cite_note-comparison-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British trade union, 1940–1989

APEX

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Association of Professional, Executive, Clerical and Computer Staff | |
|  | |
| Predecessor | [Association of Women Clerks and Secretaries](/wiki/Association_of_Women_Clerks_and_Secretaries "Association of Women Clerks and Secretaries") [National Union of Clerks](/wiki/National_Union_of_Clerks "National Union of Clerks") |
| Merged into | [GMB](/wiki/GMB_(trade_union) "GMB (trade union)") |
| Founded | 1940 |
| Dissolved | 1989 |
| Headquarters | 70 [St George's Square](/wiki/St_George%27s_Square "St George's Square"), London |
| Location | * United Kingdom |
| Members | 140,292 (1980[[1]](#cite_note-comparison-1)) |
| Publication | *The Clerk* |
| Affiliations | [TUC](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress"), [LMTU](/wiki/Joint_Committee_of_Light_Metal_Trades_Unions "Joint Committee of Light Metal Trades Unions"), [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), [FIET](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Commercial,_Clerical,_Professional_and_Technical_Employees "International Federation of Commercial, Clerical, Professional and Technical Employees") |

The **Association of Professional, Executive, Clerical and Computer Staff** (**APEX**) was a [British](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") which represented [clerical](/wiki/Clerk "Clerk") and [administrative](/wiki/Business_administration "Business administration") employees.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Association_of_Professional,_Executive,_Clerical_and_Computer_Staff&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The **Clerks Union** was formed in 1890 and later was renamed as the **National Union of Clerks**. Then, following rapid growth and amalgamation with several other unions, the name was again changed to the **National Union of Clerks and Administrative Workers** (**NUCAW**) with a membership of around 40,000.

In 1940, NUCAW merged with the [Association of Women Clerks and Secretaries](/wiki/Association_of_Women_Clerks_and_Secretaries "Association of Women Clerks and Secretaries") (AWCS) to form the **Clerical and Administrative Workers' Union** (**CAWU**). The union organised in the [white-collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") sector in the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") and across the country, and had particular success in recruiting in the [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") industry. In the 1960s its membership grew rapidly, but it was less successful in the 1970s, membership increasing by 18%, while that of its rival, the [Association of Scientific, Technical and Managerial Staffs](/wiki/Association_of_Scientific,_Technical_and_Managerial_Staffs "Association of Scientific, Technical and Managerial Staffs") (ASTMS), nearly doubled.[[1]](#cite_note-comparison-1)

The union changed its name to the Association of Professional, Executive, Clerical and Computer Staff (APEX) in 1972. It was the union at the centre of the [Grunwick dispute](/wiki/Grunwick_dispute "Grunwick dispute") in the 1970s.

APEX, like its predecessors, was an [affiliated trade union](/wiki/Affiliated_trade_union "Affiliated trade union") of the British [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and was a key influence on the right wing of the Party, particularly as, until 1972, it enforced a rule preventing [communists](/wiki/Communist "Communist") from holding positions in the union. Its relations with other unions were often difficult, as it competed not only with the ASTMS for members, but also with the [National Union of Bank Employees](/wiki/National_Union_of_Bank_Employees "National Union of Bank Employees") and various [general unions](/wiki/General_union "General union"). In particular, a dispute over members at [General Accident](/wiki/General_Accident "General Accident") was referred to the [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") Disputes Committee and the fall-out led to APEX's general secretary, [Roy Grantham](/wiki/Roy_Grantham "Roy Grantham"), failing to win re-election to the [General Council of the TUC](/wiki/General_Council_of_the_TUC "General Council of the TUC").[[1]](#cite_note-comparison-1)

In 1989 APEX merged with the [GMB](/wiki/GMB_Union "GMB Union") trade union and now exists as a section within the GMB.

## Election results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Association_of_Professional,_Executive,_Clerical_and_Computer_Staff&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Election results")]

The union sponsored numerous [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") candidates, many of whom were elected:
